title: Comparatives & Superlatives
description: Compare people, places, and things using -er/-est, more/most, and irregular forms like better/best.

items.back: big → the biggest, fast → the fastest
items.example: He's the tallest in the class.
This book is more interesting than the last one.

items.front: Long adj: the most / the least (superlative)
items.back: the most exciting, the least difficult
items.example: It's the most beautiful city I've visited.
This coffee is better. It's the best in town!
Today was worse than yesterday. The worst day ever.

items.back: She's as tall as her sister.
items.example: This hotel is as comfortable as the last one.
A house is much more expensive than an apartment.
The results were better than expected.

items.prompt: This film is ___ than the book. (boring)
items.explanation: Long adjectives use 'more' for comparatives: more boring than.

items.prompt: She is ___ student in the school. (good)
items.explanation: good → better → the best. 'The best' is the irregular superlative.

items.prompt: Mount Everest is ___ mountain in the world. (high)
items.explanation: Short adjective: high → the highest. Superlatives always use 'the'.

items.prompt: A train ticket is not ___ a plane ticket. (cheap)
items.explanation: 'Not as cheap as' = less cheap. Used to show two things are not equal.

items.prompt: Today is ___ hotter than yesterday. I'm sweating!
items.explanation: 'Much' intensifies comparatives: much hotter, much more expensive.

items.sentence: It's the ___ restaurant in town — everyone loves it. (good)
This exercise is ___ than the previous one. (difficult)
Today is ___ than yesterday — I need a jacket! (cold)
